title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Palmer District Attorney Roman Kalytiak said Wednesday he hadn't decided whether to appeal Judge Smith's decision or to present the case one more time to a grand jury next week. Meanwhile, Smith said, he's keeping the Sept. 20 murder trial on his calendar. Harapat shot his friend Kenny Alcantra in February 2003 at Harapat's family home in Palmer. There's no dispute about that. Both boys were 16. Harapat initially told investigators he was handing his .44-caliber Magnum revolver to Alcantra when it discharged. But a weapons expert testified to the grand jury that because of the gun's safety mechanism, the only way to get it to fire was to pull the trigger. The grand jury in June of 2003 indicted Harapat on charges of second-degree murder, manslaughter and criminally negligent homicide. Smit…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Substation dedicated in memory of officer Anchorage Police Department K-9 officer Anthony Rollins, wearing an honor guard uniform, took part in the dedication ceremony of a police substation Wednesday. His dog's name is Jimmy Lee. The substation was named in memory and honor of the late husband of Brenda Flora-Bissell. Officer Johnathan P. Flora was killed in the line of duty while investigating a burglary at a drug addiction treatment center downtown on Sept. 8, 1975. The police substation is located downtown on Fourth Avenue inside the new Anchorage Fire Station No. 1. After Officer Flora's death, Brenda started a grass-roots movement that became Dollars for Dogs Inc., which helps fund what is now known as the department's K-9 unit. (Photo by Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News) Published: …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: XTO purchases piece of Kuparuk XTO Energy in August bought a tiny piece of the North Slope's Kuparuk River oil field, part of a larger acquisition outside Alaska from Chevron Texaco, according to Petroleum News. XTO bought one-tenth of 1 percent of Kuparuk, the state's second largest oil field, which averaged 185,921 barrels of oil per day in July, about 20 percent of the Slope's production. XTO's share will be about 200 barrels a day, said Kyle Hammond, XTO's vice president of operations for Alaska. XTO is not actively seeking properties on the North Slope, he added. The company's Middle Ground Shoal field in Cook Inlet has averaged about 3,700 barrels a day of oil production. -- Anchorage Daily News The advertisements below are not endorsed by the Anchorage Daily News. The Anchorage Dail…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Dog-park trial period extended 3 MONTHS: Mayor proposes plan that wins Assembly backing. By ANNE AURAND Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 9, 2004) Assembly Chairman Dick Traini put the idea of off-leash dog parks on the fast track after an enforcement crackdown on leash laws was announced. Click on photo to enlarge The Anchorage Assembly late Wednesday approved extending for three more months the trial period for five off-leash dog parks in the city. The move extends the trial period until Dec. 31. Mayor Mark Begich offered the solution that Assembly members unanimously backed. The decision came one night after a two-hour, packed public hearing at which praise and criticism were heaped on the city's experimental dog parks. The Assembly had intended to decide by Sept. 30 whether to…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Just last week, the same theme was sounded in a series of commercials by a shadowy political group based in Anchorage, Business Alaska. The ads, put together with the assistance of the former director of the organization that represents the Alaska factory trawler fleet, criticized Knowles for supporting the Pew Commission. Despite the one-two-punch timing of Murkowski's ads, which come right on top of those of the business group, the Murkowski campaign said it hasn't consulted with Business Alaska on the ads. It would be illegal for Business Alaska to coordinate with the Murkowski effort. Murkowski told reporters it was fishermen's objections to the Pew report that prompted her offensive. The timing is related in part to the end of the fishing season in Alaska, the senator said. "The fishi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: The evaluation began in April, said Era president Chuck Johnson. Croyle said the process should be completed within the next few months. "It's not a fun time for Era employees to have this indecision," he said. "We want to reach a decision on the matter fairly quickly." Rowan has received several proposals to buy Era, Croyle and Johnson said. Rowan, based in Houston, has three areas of operation: oil and gas well drilling; manufacturing and sale of heavy equipment and products; and fixed-wing and helicopter services. Era's airline services are an important part of Alaska's transportation, especially in rural communities. Era has hubs in Anchorage and Bethel, and flies to 24 locations in Alaska. It also offers charter and contract flight services. Era helicopters run flightseeing tours in S…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: DESCRIPTION: A traditional Yup'ik community where almost 96 percent of residents are wholly or partly Alaska Native. Commercial fishing and subsistence activities are the primary means of support. Bureau of Land Management firefighting offers some employment, and people sell grass baskets and ivory handicrafts. There is one school, attended by about 400 students. HISTORY: "Askinuk" or "Askinaghamiut" are the early Eskimo names for Hooper Bay. The village was first reported in 1878 by E.W. Nelson of the U.S. Signal Service. The 1890 Census found 138 persons living in 14 homes. The advertisements below are not endorsed by the Anchorage Daily News. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: APOC fines Masek $1,000 The Associated Press ANCHORAGE (September 8, 7:24 pm ADT) - The Alaska Public Offices Commission on Wednesday ruled State Rep. Beverly Masek broke some campaign finance laws by filing inaccurate reports and inadequate accounting of expenditures. The commission fined the Willow Republican $1,000 and ordered her to attend a campaign disclosure training session before beginning another election campaign. But the commission rejected several of its staff's recommendations, saying the rules against mixing personal and unused campaign funds are not clearly stated. APOC's staff had recommended the commission fine Masek $7,200 for illegally spending campaign and office funds on personal expenses after she withdrew more than $7,270 for alleged personal spending. The expenditu…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Armstrong has been an active explorer for oil in nearby prospects, bringing in Pioneer Natural Resources as a 70 percent owner and operator at the Oooguruk prospect, and partnering with Kerr-McGee at the Nikaitchuq prospect. Kerr-McGee spokesman John Christiansen said his company now holds the largest interest in Tuvaaq, although state records last week still showed Armstrong as owning all of the leases alone. "We expect to operate the exploratory well that is currently planned," Christiansen said. "We will have the largest interest in the unit but the exact interests (of the owners) has not been determined at this point." The 14,561-acre Tuvaaq unit includes seven state oil and gas leases sold in 1997. Armstrong has told the state it plans to drill its first of three wells this winter, wi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Dr. Laura Johnston received the American Academy of Nurse Practitioners Award for Excellence as nurse practitioner advocate for Alaska. Johnston, consulting physician at the University of Alaska Anchorage Student Health Center, offers consultation and guidance to advanced nurse practitioner providers at the UAA clinic. She also offers primary care to students. Susan Sullivan has been named the new director for Victims for Justice. Eight athletes from Special Olympics Alaska will participate in the 2005 Special Olympics World Winter Games from Feb. 26-March 5 in Nagano, Japan. They are: alpine skiing -- Stephanie Palinski (Anchorage); cross-country skiing -- Richard Champion (Seward); figure skating -- Alyssa Tucker (Anchorage); snowboarding -- Shannon Sullivan (Anchorage), Amy Murrills (Fa…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Police believe the 20-year-old was drinking when the brawl broke out at Southwood Manor Mobile Home Park on Brayton Drive and the 15-year-old stabbed him in the arm. The 20-year-old then turned on the 15-year-old and kicked him. The two knew each other casually, Davis said. Their names are not being released because the case is still under investigation. They were treated and released from local hospitals. The 20-year-old was arrested on an outstanding warrant. This is the second stabbing at the mobile home park in recent weeks. On Aug. 5, Ian Pilcher, 19, was stabbed in the back. No one was arrested in the earlier stabbing because the victim declined to cooperate, police said. -- Anchorage Daily News KOLIGANEK Caribou hunters from Montana, Maryland named as crash victims The two men kille…

summary: Trek to Tech Parents' school options give 'a day at the mall' a whole new meaning By KATIE PESZNECKER Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 8, 2004) Highland Tech High School takes up part of the refurbished Boniface Center in East Anchorage. (Photo by JIM LAVRAKAS / Anchorage Daily News) Highland Tech High School history and technology teacher John Trampush leads a discussion about character building on the first day of classes at the school. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Alaska Knifeworks is among the businesses that would benefit, owner David Summers said. He employs four people at his downtown shop. "Every dollar counts when you're a small business and you're trying to grow," Summers said. Business advocates say the tax discourages people from owning a business or investing in more expensive, modern equipment. Supporters said the expected financial loss to the city could be made up from increased sales tax revenue because more people would have incentive to start or expand a business. "I personally believe that if you give incentives to businesses ... it will come back even more than the cut in the business personal property taxes," said Linda Thomas, of the chamber's Government Affairs Committee. Advocates also expect tax payments from the opening of th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: DESCRIPTION: A fishing-industry center and home to a number of federal offices with services focused on Aleutian transportation and wildlife protection. Because of its central location and modern airport, Cold Bay serves as the regional center for air transportation on the Alaska Peninsula and as an international hub for private aircraft. Subsistence and recreational fishing and hunting are a part of the local culture. Fewer than one in five residents are Alaska Native or part Native. There is one school, attended by about 15 students. HISTORY: Archaeological sites dating to the last ice age indicate the area around Cold Bay was once inhabited by a large Native population. It was used by European hunters and trappers throughout the 19th century. During World War II, Cold Bay was the site o…

title: More trails or better trails? The builders' dilemma
summary: WINNER CREEK ROUTE -- Aging athlete Jim Jager is one of those people prone to criticize some Anchorage trails as overbuilt these days. Too wide. Too smooth. Too civilized. He is not the only one to voice such concerns either. With the Nordic Skiing Association of Anchorage busy fixing the Tour of Anchorage Trail, more than a few words of complaint have been heard: "They're trying to turn everything into Kincaid Park!" "They're ripping out all of the tree roots!" "They're destroying the single track!'' "They've ground the trail into talcum powder!'' The latter complaint does, of course, prompt a question: Why is it that rough and rooty, single track is seen as a challenge, whereas deep, loose dirt, which will harden into a nice firm surface after a few rains, isn't? Yes, parts of the tour t…

summary: Who: Leonard Hyde and Jon Rubini developed the property through their company, JL Properties Inc., said Naomi Fuller, co-owner of the building with Richard Fuller, Hyde and Rubini. The Fullers own many of the tenant companies. General contractor for the design/build project is Davis Constructors and Engineers Inc. of Anchorage. Davis project manager Nicole Leary said major subcontractors include Anchorage businesses RIM Architects, RIM Design, DOWL Engineers, BBFM Engineers Inc., Alcan Electrical & Engineering Inc., Hay Zietlow & Associates, Superior Plumbing & Heating Inc., Alaska Sheet Metal Inc., Arctic Glazing Contractors, Parker Masonry & Tile, U-Steel Inc. and Universal Roofing. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Judge orders state to refund nonresident commercial fishermen By MATT VOLZ, Associated Press Writer ANCHORAGE, Alaska (September 5, 2:18 pm ADT) - State prosecutors are likely to challenge a judge's order to refund with interest thousands of out-of-state commercial fishermen who paid three times more for their annual permits than Alaskan fishermen. The ruling by Superior Court Judge Peter Michalski of Anchorage orders the state to pay plaintiffs in a 20-year-old class action lawsuit the difference plus interest for what they should have been charged based on a complex formula designed by the courts. The amount of the refund and the number of plaintiffs eligible has yet to be calculated by the state, assistant attorney general Robert Nauheim said. Earlier estimates have put the cost to Alas…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: The Democrats walked out because there were unresolved problems with four bills on the calendar and they didn't want to get steamrolled by the committee Republicans, said Bill Wicker, spokesman for Jeff Bingaman, ranking Democrat on the Energy Committee. Wicker said it had nothing to do with Murkowski's tough election battle against former Alaska Democratic Gov. Tony Knowles. "Our problems with the legislation have nothing whatsoever to do with Alaska politics," Wicker said. Because one Republican on the committee did not make it to the meeting, the walkout by the two Democrats present, Bingaman of New Mexico and Tim Johnson of South Dakota, prevented the quorum the committee needed to take action on amendments and send the bills to the Senate floor. The upshot will likely be that the comm…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Rental car center may win agency financing AIRPORT: AIDEA would sell bonds, own building, developer says. By RICHARD RICHTMYER Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 2, 2004) Pfeffer (Photo by Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge A state industrial development agency is considering issuing $36 million in revenue bonds to pay for construction of a rental car center at Anchorage's international airport. The idea is to build a four-level building that would house the rental car companies' ticket counters, provide indoor parking and be equipped with car washes, vacuuming stations and fuel pumps to service the rental cars on site, said Mark Pfeffer, an Anchorage developer who is heading up the project. Pfeffer has been working with a consortium of rental car companies…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: DESCRIPTION: A community with a strong traditional Kinugmiut Eskimo whaling culture. More than 90 percent of the population is Alaska Native. Ancient songs, dances and customs are still practiced. In the summer Little Diomede residents travel between the two villages in large traditional skin boats. The economy is based on subsistence hunting and fishing, trapping, Native arts and crafts and some mining. A private reindeer herd is managed out of Wales and local residents are employed to assist in the harvest. The village is accessed by air and sea only. There is one school, attended by about 45 students. HISTORY: A burial mound of the Birnirk culture (A.D. 500-900) was discovered near Wales and is now a national landmark. In 1827 the Russian Navy reported the Eskimo villages of "Eidamoo" n…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Kathi Johns can vouch for almost half of them. She's been working the rat races since 1983. "I spent my honeymoon here 22 years ago,'' she said. The fair without rat races would be like an election without attack ads that materialize from seemingly nowhere with seemingly no one's knowledge. Plenty of fun regardless, but so much more fun with the rats. The rat races involve neither rats nor racing. It involves a gerbil and a roulette wheel with about 50 spokes, each painted one of seven colors. Each spoke has a hole for the gerbil to dive into, and people bet on what color the gerbil chooses. But forget semantics; it's still rodent antics. Some of those antics played out this year in Juneau, where lawmakers expanded the list of legal charitable games to include those involving rodents and r…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Woman shot after firing handgun at Alaska State Troopers By DAN JOLING, Associated Press Writer ANCHORAGE (September 1, 10:30 am ADT) - A woman threatening suicide near Fairbanks fired a handgun at Alaska State Troopers and was shot in the nose. Margaret Mary Patrisso, 40, was shot once Tuesday with a bullet that severed the tip of her nose, said trooper spokesman Greg Wilkinson. The shooting occurred after seven hours of negotiations. The woman fired the handgun at least once in the direction of troopers and three officers returned fire. Wilkinson said the circumstances had the potential for far more injury. "It was a gift of God that this woman is alive and still walking and talking and no troopers were hit," he said. Troopers received a call shortly after 1:30 p.m. that a woman was thre…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: DESCRIPTION: A traditional Alutiiq settlement whose residents practice a commercial fishing and subsistence lifestyle and utilize salmon, halibut, seal, sea lion, clams, crab and deer. Alaska Natives comprise more than three-fourths of the inhabitants. The economy is based primarily on fishing (17 residents hold commercial permits). There are very few year-round employment positions. There is one school, attended by about 20 students. About 40 percent of homes are used only seasonally. HISTORY: Because of the many artifacts recovered, the area is thought to have been inhabited for at least 2,000 years. Russian fur traders frequented the island in the mid-1700s. The bay was named for Peter Larsen, an Unga Island furrier, hunter and guide. In the early 1800s, there was a tannery in Uyak Bay.…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Running for U.S. Senate Topic: Poverty in America 2004 U.S. Senate Candidate Q & A (Published: September 5, 2004) Murkowski Sykes (Photo by Bradly J. Boner) Knowles (SEANNA O'SULLIVAN / AP) Click on photo to enlarge Last week, the Census Bureau reported that the number of Americans living in poverty or lacking health care coverage rose for the third straight year in 2003. The national poverty rate rose to 12.5 percent, up from 12.1 percent in 2002. Women, the hardest hit, saw their earnings decline for the first time since 1999. By the end of 2003, 12.9 million children lived in poverty. Meanwhile, 45 million Americans were without health insurance last year, an increase to 15.6 percent from 15.2 percent in 2002. The Daily News asked candidates for the U.S. Senate to comment on these issue…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Derelict faces eve of destruction KUSKOKWIM ST.: Fed-up neighbors say owner never made fixes ordered by city. By ANNE AURAND Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 5, 2004) Neighbors living near a boarded-up house at 1830 Kuskokwim St. in the Airport Heights subdivision have asked the city for help. (Photo by Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Steve MacCarthy's miniature Doberman pinscher wandered into a neighbor's yard last month and came home convulsing from a drug overdose. The property where Max got into trouble, 1830 Kuskokwim St. in City View, was identified by Anchorage police as a methamphetamine lab in 1999. Police have responded to about 50 calls there since 1997, a department spokesman said. On July 13 a woman died in the house from a gunshot wound. …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Several new monitors will give a better picture of earthquakes beneath the northeast corner of the mountain, filling in kind of a hole in coverage, and providing a more accurate measurement of the total energy produced, Waythomas said. Three new global positioning system devices will also show whether Spurr's surface has been rising. "We'll get real-time data to our office here in Anchorage that will tell us about ground motion," he said. "If the volcano begins to inflate because of the movement of magma, we'll be able to detect that too." The volcano, one of 27 actively monitored by the observatory along the Aleutian Arc, last erupted in 1992 from a vent in Crater Peak, dusting Anchorage with a layer of fine ash. This summer, hundreds of earthquakes were detected beneath the volcano's mai…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "We were tracking it on the Division of Elections Web site, and we definitely noticed it," said state House candidate Gabrielle LeDoux of Kodiak. "My numbers would go up and then they'd go down sometimes. "I never tried to get an explanation as long as we were ahead." LeDoux, the mayor of the Kodiak Island Borough, defeated appointed incumbent Dan Ogg and challenger Lorne "Lonnie" White to win the Republican primary for District 36. She faces no opposition in the November general election. Elections Director Laura Glaiser said the problem stemmed from workers uploading results to the Internet. Those workers forgot to include absentee votes in the data before putting it on the Web site. The division's computer system requires the absentee votes be manually selected for inclusion in the over…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Alaska Digest STATE FAIR GETS ROLLING (Published: August 26, 2004) Kevin Beagley races against the clock Wednesday afternoon to paint his family's vending booth at the Alaska State Fair in Palmer to prepare for today's opening. The Beagley family's Sidelines booth will sell T-shirts to fairgoers. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge MAT-SU Change means borough students must register at school this week Any student planning to attend a public school in the Matanuska-Susitna Borough should go to the school by Friday to register, district spokeswoman Kim Floyd said Wednesday. "We saw a lot of new students in the past three days," Floyd said. "However, we're not seeing a lot of returning students." In past years, returning students haven't needed to show up bef…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "We chose to take on this fight, knowing that we would likely be outspent 10 to one, that it was not a level playing field, and that the going would be tough," Miller said. "But our supporters were amazing in their dedication, and I cannot thank them enough." Meanwhile, former Gov. Tony Knowles, who cruised to victory in the Democratic primary, announced he won support from hundreds of Alaska Republicans. Murkowski's campaign had hoped Miller would show up at Election Central at the Egan Center on Tuesday night and raise her hand on the victory podium in a public show of unity. Instead, he stayed at his hotel near the airport, then drove home to North Pole on Wednesday without even talking to Murkowski. "They've left cell phone messages for each other," Murkowski spokeswoman Kristin Pugh s…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Environmental groups object to pollock eco-label By MARY PEMBERTON, Associated Press Writer ANCHORAGE, Alaska (August 25, 6:00 pm ADT) - Several environmental groups are objecting to Alaska's pollock fishery - the largest fishery in the United States - getting approval for an eco-label. Oceana, Greenpeace International, the National Environmental Trust and Alaska Oceans Program are objecting to the Bering Sea and Gulf of Alaska pollock fisheries making the grade for an eco-label from the Marine Stewardship Council, an international nonprofit that promotes sustainable fisheries. The pollock fisheries were approved for the eco-label by Scientific Certification Systems Inc., of Emeryville, Calif., hired by MSC to evaluate the $750 million fishery. The certification is being sought by At-Sea P…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Masek fined $1,000 but cleared on some counts FUNDS: Legislator was accused of improperly spending official funds. By DON HUNTER Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 9, 2004) Masek Click on photo to enlarge The Alaska Public Offices Commission fined state Rep. Bev Masek $1,000 Wednesday for improperly spending some campaign and legislative office funds on household expenses and to repay loans, but it cleared her of other alleged violations. Masek was ordered to attend a campaign disclosure training session before running for office again. She lost in the Republican primary to challenger Mark Neuman. The fine is a fraction of the $7,200 penalty the APOC's staff investigators had recommended last month. The investigation grew out of a complaint filed more than a year ago by a former Ma…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Gillam and a passenger had died in the crash; four other passengers survived for 33 days in the winter wilderness before being rescued. Gillam and Earhart had been flying the same type of plane -- the Lockheed Electra. The researchers wanted to know if parts from the Alaska wreckage matched those they had found earlier on a South Pacific island. Earhart's disappearance at age 39 remains one of America's great mysteries and the subject of continuing searches of the Pacific. She had set numerous flying records before she began her final flight May 20, 1937, from Oakland, Calif. She made it as far as New Guinea, where she took off on July 2 for tiny Howland Island on a 2,556-mile flight. She and her navigator, Fred Noonan, never made it to the tiny atoll southwest of Hawaii. On Nikumaroro Isl…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Englishman rescued after rowboat capsizes at sea By PETER PORCO, The Associated Press ANCHORAGE (August 24, 2:18 pm ADT) - A 39-year-old Englishman rowing alone across the northern Pacific Ocean was rescued about 600 miles south of Dutch Harbor early Monday after his small boat capsized, the U.S. Coast Guard said. Mick Dawson of Lincolnshire, U.K., was about two-thirds of the way across the ocean from Choshi, Japan, when his 23-foot rowing boat was hit by something in dead-calm seas. Dawson was headed to San Francisco. "Literally the boat was picked up and flipped," he told the BBC in a radio interview aboard a cargo vessel that picked him up. "No way it was a wave." Dawson doubted a whale had upended his boat. In the more than three months he'd been rowing east on the open ocean, about 70…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: A disproportionate number of traffic accidents occur over Labor Day weekend both nationally and locally, according to Mary Rodman-Lopez, grants administrator for the Alaska Highway Safety Office. So far this year there have been 61 fatal crashes on the Alaska roadway system, causing 63 deaths, Rodman-Lopez said. For all of 2003, there were 85, causing 96 deaths. About 37 percent of the 2003 fatal accidents involved alcohol, troopers said. Col. Julia Grimes, director of the troopers, said in a written statement that many people would probably be headed to fairs, derbies and other special events this weekend. She encouraged them to drive safely, designate a driver if they plan to drink and to buckle up. Police officers around the state, including those in Wasilla, Palmer, Homer, Kenai, Soldo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: In hearings last month, most people who spoke criticized the proposal as a rollback of clean water standards that could hurt the quality and image of Alaska's wild fish. They asked for more time to comment, noting that the fishing season is under way and the Alaskans who might be most affected are busy. Fish and Game Commissioner Kevin Duffy said Thursday that staff from his agency, the Department of Natural Resources and DEC met this week to discuss the mixing zone proposal after his department and DNR requested an interagency consultation. Fish and Game is reserving judgment until after the public comment period concludes, Duffy said, adding that he doesn't want to engage in "a back and forth banter" about it in the press. "This is a huge public policy call," Duffy said. Duffy spoke in A…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: National Transportation Safety Board investigator Clint Johnson looks over the wreckage of a downed plane Wednesday near the Wolf Lake airstrip in Palmer. The pilot, 43-year-old Marty Palmer, died in the crash. When Palmer took off in the home-built airplane, he cleared the forest at the end of the runway but then banked to the right and plummeted into the trees. Investigators say the plane suffered so much damage it may be impossible to tell what caused the crash. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Heroic river rescue lends solace to Knik tragedy RECOGNITION: Helicopter crew honored for plucking surviving family to safety. By ZAZ HOLLANDER Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 1, 2004) The three-man crew of a forestry helicopter searches the Knik River for Clifford Buswell, 3, after an accident on the river on Aug. 14. They had already rescued Clifford's mother and younger brother. (Photo by Stephen Nowers / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge PALMER -- Norm McDonald leaned out of the state Division of Forestry's firefighting helicopter, hovering just six inches above the roiling silt-gray waters of the Knik River. It was three Saturdays ago. Just minutes earlier, 24-year-old Jennifer Buswell was filling her water tank at a riverbank dirt launch, as she often did, he…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "Only then will we be able to answer the question of whether we really have a project," Bridgman told the board earlier this month. At that point, the state would have to make a major commitment of $200 million to $300 million to do more detailed engineering studies, he said. Those studies would take two years to do, and by mid-2007 the state would be in a position to decide whether to actually build the project, which would cost about $10 billion and be in operation in early 2010. The Legislature appropriated $300,000 so that the authority could do an initial feasibility study. Heinze and his staff reviewed progress on the report, required by a ballot measure approved by Alaska voters in the November 2002 general election. The report will be published in September and will include the res…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Misdemeanors are punishable by up to a year in jail. In the criminal courts, they're called third-party custodians or TPCs, usually friends or relatives of a defendant who is awaiting trial and prefers to do so out of jail. Occasionally a defendant who can afford it hires someone to do the job. Third-party custodians must be approved by a judge. They agree to immediately report any misbehavior and to make sure the defendant shows up in court when scheduled. Often the custodians must promise to keep the defendant within sight or sound 24 hours a day and enforce a string of bail conditions, including no drinking or drugs, no contact with witnesses or victims and, most importantly, no criminal behavior. In three of the five cases filed Monday, the defendant in question was charged with commit…

He owns Alaska Horsemen Trail Adventures in Cooper Landing. (Photo by Daron Dean / Anchorage Daily News) Kime moved beyond drinking and family trouble to do what he loves most: exist among horses. He owns Alaska Horsemen Trail Adventures in Cooper Landing. Floyd Green keeps a close eye on steaks, baked potatoes and acorn squash being cooked by the campfire. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Last year's statewide salmon harvest was in Alaska's top 15 as far as sheer number of fish caught. But it also was the lowest-value catch since 1976, according to the department. The additional pink salmon don't bode well for the market, said seafood industry analyst Chris McDowell. "Unless there is a major surplus reduction buy from USDA (the Department of Agriculture), the short version of a long story is that we're producing about 3.5 million cases of canned pinks a year, and we're selling about 3 million," he said. Gov. Frank Murkowski asked the Department of Agriculture last week to buy $30 million in surplus canned pink salmon. Murkowski spokesman John Manly said the request is pending. The state also is expecting a larger chum harvest than last year. As for the big-money salmon, the…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Alaska State Fair, noon-10 p.m. weekdays, 10 a.m.-10 p.m. weekends, through Sept. 6, Alaska State Fairgrounds, Palmer; $8, $5 seniors/youths, free children under 5 (admission; some events not included). The fair schedule is elsewhere in this section. The route to Palmer has changed because of the new bridges at the Parks-Glenn intersection. After crossing the Knik River Bridge, fair-goers must stay in the right lane and watch for the Palmer exit sign. Approaching the fair, turn on Inner Springer Loop South and follow the signs to the Red Parking Lot. The Alaska Railroad will offer service to the fair today-Sunday and Sept. 4-5. Cost, which includes fair admission, is $18-$45, depending on age. Children under 2 are free. Call 265-2494 for a schedule. Daily bus service from Anchorage is avai…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Duck breeding program makes a splash STELLER'S EIDER: The species has declined in Alaska and worldwide. By MARY PEMBERTON The Associated Press (Published: August 30, 2004) Steller's eiders have new breeding habitat at the Alaska SeaLife Center in Seward. (Photo by HEIDI WEINGARTZ / Alaska SeaLife Center) Click on photo to enlarge Researchers at the Alaska SeaLife Center are poking into the private lives of the Steller's eider, a rare sea duck that is disappearing from its nesting grounds in Alaska. Ten males and seven females were collected from the Alaska Peninsula and Aleutian Islands in 2003 and placed in a large outdoor pen at the Seward center as part of a federally funded captive-breeding program. Researchers have observed the sea ducks for a year. To help get them in the mood this s…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: But this idea may be even more controversial than previous ones because it puts more social services downtown in an area that already has many. Tim Marshall, Cornell director of development for the western region, pictures the annex remodeled, with grass, trees, shrubs and a basketball court outside for 11-to-17-year-old boys who either have psychiatric problems or are developmentally disabled. "There will be no razor wire, no fencing. It will not look like a correctional facility. If anything, it will look like a hospital," he said. "It will be pretty." The idea is to bring home some of Alaska's children who are currently out of state receiving treatment, Marshall said. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: The proposed Alaska Office of Faith-Based and Community Initiatives and the governor's advisory council would function within the state Department of Health and Social Services. The office will employ four people to work with social service agencies in various roles, including assistance in grant writing and technical help. Small grants will be available to help agencies develop programs. Concerns that the line between church and state would be blurred have stymied the Bush initiative, which was launched as an effort to open government programs to churches, synagogues and other religious organizations. Al Sundquist, president of the Alaska chapter of Americans United for Separation of Church and State, said he has the same concerns about the Alaska office. "Our country has been getting alo…

title: More trails or better trails? The builders' dilemma
summary: WINNER CREEK ROUTE -- Aging athlete Jim Jager is one of those people prone to criticize some Anchorage trails as overbuilt these days. Too wide. Too smooth. Too civilized. He is not the only one to voice such concerns either. With the Nordic Skiing Association of Anchorage busy fixing the Tour of Anchorage Trail, more than a few words of complaint have been heard: "They're trying to turn everything into Kincaid Park!" "They're ripping out all of the tree roots!" "They're destroying the single track!'' "They've ground the trail into talcum powder!'' The latter complaint does, of course, prompt a question: Why is it that rough and rooty, single track is seen as a challenge, whereas deep, loose dirt, which will harden into a nice firm surface after a few rains, isn't? Yes, parts of the tour t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "I noticed that the stern was getting lower in the water," Hess told the Chilkat Valley News. Hess' distress calls were picked up by the Chilkat Express, a high-speed catamaran and one of three vessels that make up Chilkat Cruises. The company provides daily crossings between Haines and Skagway, separated by 350 road miles but 35 minutes by catamaran. As Hess and Palmer waited for rescue, their boat ended up stern-down in the water. Palmer, who was wearing a life jacket, was washed overboard, while Hess clung to the bow for safety. He was unable to reach his life jacket, stashed below deck. "It happened so quick," Palmer said. "It was a struggle just to breathe, let alone hang on." Palmer said she was not certain they would be rescued. "I just followed Gary's instructions. He saved me from…

summary: Residents did manage to save their only ambulance. Then the fire swept over the mostly steel building, trapping all other equipment inside, including a new tanker truck, a pump truck and a pickup. The volunteers and nearly the whole village could only watch. "Everyone just backed off," Sonsalla said. "It was just too much." Several small explosions soon followed. Residents speculated the fire finding its way to the fuel tanks in the vehicles had caused them. The village health clinic, which stands next to the fire department building, was saved by firefighters using an extra, small hose that was not stored in the fire department building. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: U.S. Coast Guard saves Englishman DUTCH HARBOR: Six hundred miles south, far from his capsized boat, Mick Dawson was in a life raft. By PETER PORCO Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 24, 2004) Mick Dawson aboard Mrs. D's Viking Spirit. His second attempt to row the 23-foot boat across the Pacific failed Monday. (Photo courtesy of Mick Dawson ) Click on photo to enlarge A 39-year-old Englishman rowing alone across the northern Pacific Ocean was rescued about 600 miles south of Dutch Harbor early Monday after his small boat capsized for unknown reasons, the U.S. Coast Guard said. Mick Dawson of Lincolnshire, U.K., was about two-thirds of the way across the ocean from Choshi, Japan, to his destination of San Francisco, according to the Web site of the Ocean Rowing Society, a London-based…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: A. If one of the "sloshed" employees crashes his car on the way home, your company may find itself on the losing end of a liability lawsuit. Equally as troublesome, your company's beer-stocked refrigerator makes it easy for those sliding down the slope into alcoholism to drink at work, potentially increasing both work problems and personal tragedy. You face several challenges here. First, while you got your job because of your husband's personal relationship with the owner and because we often turn to our spouses for advice on tricky workplace problems, you may occasionally ask your husband for his opinion. Once you have his advice, however, remember you work the job and he sits outside the situation as friend and spouse. Thus, while he can counsel you, you make the final decision. Second,…

"If we get a truckload of bananas, we can call Bean's and say, 'Could you put banana bread, banana pie and banana pudding on your menu this week?' " said Susannah Morgan, executive director of the Food Bank. (Photo by Evan R. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Airport officials are scrambling to implement plans to stabilize the dike that runs between the river and the float pond, where floatplanes are kept. "There's been a significant amount of erosion on the embankment between the airport and the river," airport manager Allan Heese said recently. "Primarily the float pond is at risk right now." Although the cut is downstream from the dike, experts say the change in the river will have upstream effects as well. The land is causing the river to entrench itself and create a meandering effect, they said. Heese said the airport is not in an emergency, but he has started the process of getting appropriate permits to stabilize the dike. Heese is looking to place 500- to 1,000-pound boulders against the dike to prevent the river from eroding it and aff…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "Hopefully through this effort we get communities that, one way or another, can sustain the services being provided and the infrastructure being bought with federal or state dollars," Black said. One or more of the villages may stumble as they learn to better manage their finances and fuel, Black said. "But whatever improvements they can make ... is a great step forward, in my mind." Many remote villages purchase their fuel in bulk and have it delivered by barge. Many communities need at least 10,000 gallons per year to run a water treatment plant, heat buildings and perhaps sell small amounts to home- owners. Larger villages with diesel-powered electrical generators may need 150,000 gallons per year. At this year's price, which Black said was around $2.50 a gallon, most villages can't pur…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Tiny Gustavus faces big challenges The Associated Press JUNEAU (August 22, 2:00 pm ADT) - A sign hangs over the heads of Gustavus City Council members during meetings. It reads, "We hope our ship comes in before the dock rots." The sign is no joke. At a time when state support is making life tough on small towns, Gustavus has a serious problem with its dock, which is the major source of supplies from the world beyond the remote sandy shore near Glacier Bay National Park. The challenges faced by the fishermen, artists, sawyers, tour operators and national park employees of Gustavus go far beyond a deteriorating dock. Because of massive state budget cuts, the existence of several small towns incorporated over the last two decades is at serious risk, said Bill Rolfzen, a local government spec…

summary: • Wells Fargo hired Susan Kernes as its Alaska contributions manager and community support representative, responsible for its charitable contributions program, volunteer event coordination and community support programs and initiatives. summary: Andrew Van Chau, a BP spokesman, said the computer technicians' work is different than that of the other employees covered under the existing PACE contract, and they work on both the east and west sides of the Prudhoe Bay oil field. The PACE contract they want to join covers employees who work exclusively on the west side, Van Chau said. "BP has bargained in good faith and offered a number of proposals," he said. "We're very disappointed that the union chose to take this action rather than continue to negotiate. We wanted to continue discussions."
